shower.thought: We spend our whole lives learning how to be ourselves, but by then we're too tired to enjoy it.
The people who hurt you most are usually the ones who showed you who you really are.
Time moves slower when you're waiting for something to end than when you're waiting for it to begin, but both feel infinite.
You can't miss something you never had, which is why potential is more painful than loss.
Everyone's a main character in their own story, which means everyone's also a background character in yours.
The older you get, the more you realize that most arguments were never about being right—they were about being heard.
We call it "being ourselves" but most of the time we're just performing the version of ourselves that got the most applause.
Forgetting someone is like deleting a file—it only feels gone until something reminds you it was there all along.
You don't actually fear the dark. You fear what the dark might be hiding, which is just another way of fearing the unknown you.
The worst lies aren't the ones others tell you—they're the ones you tell yourself while maintaining perfect eye contact in the mirror.
Nostalgia is just grief wearing a smile.
Most people aren't afraid of failing—they're afraid of finding out they're capable of trying.
You measure your progress against where you've been, but you measure your worth against where others are.
The people who seem most sure of themselves are usually just better at ignoring their own doubts.
Happiness isn't found—it's accidentally discovered while you're busy complaining about something else.
We're all just pretending we know what we're doing, and the scariest part is when you realize the people we trusted most were pretending too.
Your regrets are proof you learned something, but that doesn't make them hurt less.
The hardest conversations are the ones where both people are right, and you have to choose between being right and being close.
We spend decades running from who we were, only to realize that person had answers the current version keeps searching for.
Every ending feels sudden even when you saw it coming from miles away.

motivational.thought: The obstacle is not blocking your path — it is the path. — Unknown
Success is not final, failure is not fatal: it is the courage to continue that counts. — Winston Churchill
You cannot hate yourself into a version of yourself you love. — Lindo Bacon
The only way out is through. — Robert Frost
Discipline is choosing between what you want now and what you want most. — Abraham Lincoln
Your excuses are investments in a future you don't want. — Unknown
The man who says it cannot be done should not interrupt the man doing it. — Chinese Proverb
Clarity comes from engagement, not thought. — Marie Forleo
Small progress is still progress. — Unknown
Fear is the cost of caring about something. — Unknown
You are not behind. You are not ahead. You are exactly where you need to be. — Unknown
The quality of your life is determined by the quality of the questions you ask yourself. — Tony Robbins
Regret for the things we did can be tempered by time; it is regret for the things we did not do that is inconsolable. — Sydney Smith
Your comfort zone is a lovely place, but nothing grows there. — Unknown
The worst day of your life is still worth living. — Unknown
What we resist persists. What we accept can transform. — Carl Jung
You don't need permission to become who you want to be. — Unknown
The greatest glory in living lies not in never falling, but in rising every time we fall. — Nelson Mandela
Every expert was once a beginner who refused to quit. — Unknown
The only person you are destined to become is the person you decide to be. — Ralph Waldo Emerson

darkjoke.thought:
My therapist asked why I sabotage my own happiness.
I said, "Someone has to, or I'd feel like I'm not putting in the effort."

I tried to make friends at the gym.
Turns out "spotting" someone doesn't mean you should follow them to their car.

My dad said he'd always be there for me.
He wasn't. But the child support checks were, so he was kind of there in spirit.

I got COVID, and my friends finally stopped texting me.
I miss them so much, I've almost fully recovered.

My ex-therapist said I had unhealthy attachment patterns.
I've been calling her office 14 times a day to discuss it.

I told my mom I was suicidal, and she said, "Don't be dramatic."
Turns out that's the worst possible pep talk.

My dating profile says "looking for someone to fix me."
Three therapists matched with me, and even they swiped left.

I tried to have a good day today.
My brain said, "You have a quota to meet," and ruined it on principle.

My doctor told me I have the liver of a 60-year-old.
I'm 29, so either I'm dying early or finally ahead of schedule.

I asked my coworker how she stays so positive.
She said, "Antidepressants and denial."
I think I finally found my soulmate.

My kid asked why I'm always tired.
I said, "Because I'm keeping you alive," and now they think I'm a martyr.

I've been taking antidepressants for five years.
My baseline unhappiness is now just premium unhappiness.

My dentist said I grind my teeth at night.
I said, "At least something productive happens while I sleep."

I told my friend I was having a mental health crisis.
He said, "Me too! We should hang out!"
We haven't spoken in three months.

I texted my mom "I love you" and she responded "Thanks."
That's the most honest conversation we've ever had.

My credit score is exactly as depressed as I am.
We really grew together.

I keep a diary of my failures.
It's basically a very long text file that autosaves.

My partner asked if I wanted to talk about our relationship.
I said, "Would you leave if I said no?"
Turns out the answer is more complicated than I hoped.

My alarm clock judges me every morning.
By the fifth snooze, I can feel its disappointment.

I went to a self-help seminar called "Be Your Best Self."
The instructor kicked me out for heckling the affirmations.
Honestly, it was the most honest moment of my life.
